speaker_turns.speaker_role: Operator: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to the ROCKWOOL Report of First Half Year 2022. Today, I'm
speaker_turns.text:
pleased to present CEO, Jens Birgersson; CFO, Kim Junge Andersen; and IR, Thomas Harder. For the first part
of this call, all participants will be in a listen-only mode; and afterwards, there will be a question-and-answer
session. As a reminder, this conference call is being recorded.
I will now turn the presentation over to your host. Please begin.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Director-Group Treasury & Investor Relations, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to the ROCKWOOL A/S conference call regarding the results for the first half
year of 2022. My name is Thomas Harder, I'm Director of Group Treasury and Investor Relations of ROCKWOOL
A/S. Today, I'm pleased to present CEO, Jens Birgersson and CFO, Kim Junge Andersen. For the first part of this
call, all participants will be in a listen-only mode. As a reminder, this conference call is being recorded.
First, Jens Birgersson will go through our presentation and give you an update on the results for the first half year
and second quarter of 2022. Afterwards, we'll be ready to answer all your good questions. Before I hand over the
words to Jens Birgersson, I must ask you to notice slide number 2, which is the forward-looking statement. Please
be aware that this presentation contains uncertainties.
Now we can go to the next slide, which is slide number 3. Jens Birgersson, I'll now hand over the words to you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you, Thomas. Hello, everyone. It's Jens here. So, I assume you see slide 3 in front of you. So, before we
start with the numbers from the quarter – the half year, go through an update from our sustainability goals and the
progress we do there.
Starting in the top, generally I would say we are progressing quite well. We are implementing the projects and
moving forward with our technology development. So, starting on the emission intensity, that's the CO2 emission
per tonne produced, stone wool, we have all this, the Science Based Target in absolute values. But we have this
metric back from 2016 where we measure per tonne produced. We had a goal for 2022 of reducing this to 10%,
and we reached the a 16% reduction across the board in the group already last year. So, satisfactory progress.
The next goal in the top row, reclaimed material. Our goal was to reduce it by – [ph] the whole (00:03:02) – this is
the Rockcycle offering, countries that we offer to take back both product. There are many more countries that take
back non-virgin material, but this is a matter of taking back and having a comprehensive stitch of taking back our
own product and put it back into production. We have a goal of achieving 15 countries by the end of this year. We
just launched Romania and Finland. And we are now – we have launched and we are now 17 countries already in
2021. So, we are ahead of that goal also.
Energy efficiency, on the right, this is the energy efficiency of our own offices, measured in kilowatt hour per
square meter. And here, we wanted to drop 75% by 2030 and 35% by 2022. We have just completed Hungary
and France. We are 19%. Before year-end, we need to reduce the run rate on [indiscernible] (00:04:13) 35%.
I would say these projects go well, they progress well, but we lost a little bit of pace during corona. My crew
[indiscernible] (00:04:26) is still going to be meeting that goal. We might [ph] use (00:04:32) just a little bit or we
might be a little bit ahead of it, but it's progressing well and they're increasing their building renovations. We do
have the desired impact and more. So, that's good, but it looks like there's a bit of a gap so far. But I'm not too
worried about it because we kind of develop the model for how to develop, also to renovate our [indiscernible]
(00:04:57). So, that's good, and we have done it in a very evolving approach. We actually use our own
management team to do quite a bit of the conceptual plan.
Water consumption, creating fresh water. We get water very cheaply, [indiscernible] (00:04:57) free almost
everywhere. But the [indiscernible] (00:05:16) said that with so many areas of the world running dry on drinking
water, fresh water, we have an obligation to do that. We have the goal of 10% reduction by 2022, and we
achieved 15% already in 2021. And we have – our own goal is then 20% by 2030. So, we are progressing well on
that and trying to do it in a way that it doesn't cost too much. Some of these projects are closed circuits. And the
factories on that, actually they're quite expensive to make. But once you've done it, you [ph] gauge the results
from those systems (00:05:59). So, that's good. And also, it is also housekeeping to make sure it's complete to
the extent we are not – again, we're being careful with each [ph] plot (00:06:08).
Landfill waste goal 2030, reducing by 85% and 40% by 2022. We hit 50%, 51% already last year. So, that's just
well.
Safety, health and well-being, this is long – lost time incident frequency ratio. So, that's number of days per million
man hours. It has been quite a challenging year last year. Then we started off after corona and we started to ramp
up. We have some ideas why that happened. It wasn't very bad. It's definitely been the crew. This year has
started well, and we see good improvement and we hope to hang on to that. And should we say that on that
safety LTI ratio of 3.2, 3.5 that we have now is quite tough to get down. But we are working on it and it's
progressive. And as long as we don't have really serious incidents and fatalities, this is – it feels a little bit better,
but more work needed, but it is on good level.
I move to – she says – so, slide 4, H1 for six months. And top line just below €2 billion or €60 million below €2
billion. Growth in local currencies, 31%. And that – the majority of that is priced, but it's also quite good volume
growth in the first six months. It's double-digit volume growth, but the majority of the growth is priced due to this
cost inflation. The EBIT margin reached 12% for the six months, and I come back to that again when we get to the
Q2 results. Free cash flow, negative, mainly due to net working capital. And with this growth, trade receivables go
up, but also the inventory valuation going up. So, you'll see that in a couple of percentage points higher net
working capital. There's such financial effects on the loss impact there.
And then we also have the non-realized losses on the fact that we have – we're supposed to have rubles for
Russia in a normal structure and – around March. That wasn't possible to have anywhere. So, we have the
corresponding amount at the time in euros because we couldn't [indiscernible] (00:09:21) rubles anymore and we
couldn't have rubles anymore. And that is also quite a substantial impact, although it's actually not the [ph] ruble
(00:09:30) cash impact, but it's [indiscernible] (00:09:33).
Move to slide 5, Q2 highlights, €1 billion on top line in the quarter. Very big quarter for us obviously, the top line.
26% growth in local currencies. And in Q2, we saw a change in the market, and it's a very, very modest volume
growth. And a vast majority of that 26% is the price. We are seeing this happen, that we had quite a strong start of
the year. And then we have seen the realities of the high energy prices, interest rates, the war in Ukraine, what's
happening in the US. We start to see that impact volumes.
And we then move on to EBIT margin. We did raise prices according to our plan. We had quite weak – weaker
EBIT margin in Q1. And we have – in Q2, the price increases were bigger than the inflationary, [ph] mainly
(00:10:52) the energy increases, which meant that we can [ph] include (00:10:55) – we managed to claw back
some of the margins. But compared to Q1, we improved the margin to 1.9 percentage points, and we [ph] tend to
(00:11:05) have less dilution. So, we compensated the price more than inflationary pressures we had. So, we're
very pleased with that, and I will come back to the whole inflational energy cost outlook and the pricing in a bit.
And if we then move to sales going down – the slide 6, Thomas – again, about majority's price, we say two-thirds
price, one-third volume in H1. And here is the – in the Insulation business, we saw strong business across the
board. That is clear. You could see in the second quarter some markets that slowed down or stopped because of
too much inventory or changes in the market. But in – if you sum up the six months, it's just strong almost
everywhere in the Insulation business.
System [indiscernible] (00:12:19), they didn't have a good six months. Here, you see a business like Rockpanel,
very building related, having tremendously strong development. We also see how our vegetable business is doing
good. But Grodan retail, North America, very, very big comparable last year and quite a big downturn of that
business where there's not much going on at the moment, and that has a big impact.
LATAM [ph] as far a bigger piece into cost (00:12:57). It was growing. You're seeing some constraints from that
business. So, that's down. But basically you see that the federal growth businesses outside buildings that haven't
done so great, Rockfon had a flattish development in Europe, and [ph] had a (00:13:21) negative development in
Europe. So, that's the picture. So, [ph] not getting top line (00:13:25).
If you then go into Q2, so on slide 7, here, volume growth is less. Price – the price element of the top line is
almost all of it. And you see particularly Systems that had a negative development in several businesses,
although some of the businesses performed really well. Overall, again, Insulation doing well. Majority of the top
line is price.
Move on to slide 8, Western Europe generally is strong. Germany, UK, Italy, Norway did well; but it's double-digit
growth almost everywhere. In Eastern Europe and Russia, tremendously strong Eastern Europe. In Russia, we
see heavy inflation there too. We are rounding that now as we are the owners. So, we are not allowed to
operationally control if we are a owner. We do that to make sure our IT and technology space in Rockfon doesn't
end up in the wrong hands. And obviously there, you see volume slowed down as expected as the double-digit
volume slowed down, particularly value growth due to inflation and price increases. But I would say Eastern
Europe is the really strong element here.
Moving on to North America, Asia, and others, US took a tumble in Q2. Here to see before the interest rate
increases, we saw there's quite high levels of stocks here among the distributors. So, that went double-digit
negative. And then Canada had a fantastic quarter with really high growth numbers. So, that's kind of evened out.
Asia, double-digit growth, very low development. And China, still negative. And our view on the US, we need to
see another couple of quarters. We have had these stop-and-go quarters before, but don't think that there's quite
drastic increases in the interest rate and also some of the inflationary pressures. I would guess that residential
newbuild will get impact in the US.
Moving to slide 9, and this I think the rest of backdrop to our change of guidance. Basically, we have the price
planned. And somewhere towards the end of June, the [indiscernible] (00:16:50) stabilized at bit, and we went out
with our price increases for Q3 and Q4. We have done most of Q3 already communicated, but we upped the
targets a bit and then we launched our Q4 increases. So, that's what's put into play and seems to be progressing
well, those price increases like the ones before. But then towards the end of June, especially into July, we then
saw natural gas basically increased [indiscernible] (00:17:32) and electricity [indiscernible] (00:17:35) again. And
that's had a massive impact on our cost base.
Then I'll move to [indiscernible] (00:17:43) and conclude that the [indiscernible] (00:17:44) now because we still
have lead times in pricing. We're basically around a quarter and a half, two quarters ahead. We want to
communicate we can change some prices [indiscernible] (00:17:55), but we have listened to them and that's the
reason we [indiscernible] (00:17:57) expect on previous years but it's not a commodity. We don't price only to be
priced a few months out with a six weeks' notice for the same prices. So, I came to the conclusion that we've got
development, both the non-GAAP and the electrical prices all over the place. I felt that now this is quite
substantial increase we have in the market. We don't really have a base for where this is coming on. It might get
worse. It might get better. It might stay on this level. I came to the conclusion that I cannot start to shift the prices
again and therefore be more realistic to still have some price increases that are in progress. But on the overall
[indiscernible] (00:18:51), it's simply too small to compensate another €100 to €150 million of inflationary or
energy costs that now came in, so therefore we adjusted the EBIT margins at that time to [ph] 12% (00:19:12).
Should we said like for example gas in Q4, 50% hedged. On the coke, we have coverage three, four months out if
you go quarter-by-quarter but then it's quarterly and the balance it will be in three areas of our business and
having to [indiscernible] (00:19:36). So, roughly [indiscernible] (00:19:39-00:19:44) Still so much more on pricing
and still incredible with our customers.
If we move to slide 10, you'll see – I guess the number what is interesting there is the EBITDA increased over
18% in the quarter on the 25%, 26% top line. Obviously, idea is if you have a 26% top line, it's obviously 26%
more bottom line improvement. But the fact that you see 18% there it's because we managed to increase prices
more than the absolute inflationary increase. In other, we improved progress, clearly [indiscernible] (00:20:33-
00:20:38) but even these margins in absolute terms, it's a good profit if we're talking about EBITDA. If we then
move over to the margins where you don't have the dilution effects, I would say that last year in Q2, we have an
incredibly difficult comparable or a high comparable due to the [indiscernible] (00:21:04) very exceptional quarter
from the 12.9%, the recovery from the 11%. It's quite a good one. It's [indiscernible] (00:21:15) if conditions
changed very quickly at the end of – I will say in July [indiscernible] (00:21:24) of June. So, the comparable
difficulty the year before but the level in Q3 is not too bad. We're quite satisfied with our [indiscernible] (00:21:36).
If we move on to slide 11 and we then go into the businesses, you see again when you look at the Systems
division, the comparable, it was an extraordinary strong quarter, maybe €30 million would have been a more
average comparable if you average out the quarters last year, so that will result in, let's say, a mix effect but very
pleasing on the Insulation business how the energy price increases and the rest have been actually passed on
quite well and we have improved the EBIT margin compared to a year back. And the year back, we had also quite
high margins because we had a very low cost base after the Corona and now it's more volume and price
[indiscernible] (00:22:31). And then on the distribution, lower volume, cost absorption effect and also mix effect,
the biggest effect on a comparable solution, 3.1% year before, it's abnormally high under 10%. We need to work
out because that's still low in our business.
If we move on to slide 12, we have postponed some things, for example, the Grodan lines because now the
demand is not as high. In North America, we have some investments in Canada. But apart from that, most of the
investment that hasn't happen is because [indiscernible] (00:23:20) has already been stopped in approvals. For
example, the ones in France and the general load, capacity utilization of the factories which [indiscernible]
(00:23:33) so we have to choose between delivering and doing some sustainability projects and other projects but
still in Q2 with almost €30 million in sustainability investments. We are progressing. It's not like we sit around the
table until [indiscernible] (00:23:53) CapEx. That's not what we do. This is more a natural outcome of the business
[ph] in this direction (00:23:57).
If we move on to slide 13, the operating cash flow is not all [indiscernible] (00:24:06). It is down and the story is
basically that net working capital is [indiscernible] (00:24:16) but then also based on the large loss of – cash great
loss is impacting on this cash flow. So, that's the majority of the decline is about the non-cash item impact.
Moving on to guidance, sales, compared to first-half here, we see lower activity. Personally, I believe that the risk
of recession, both in North America and Europe is high now. The US have had two quarters with negative GDP
growth. Although employment markets are very good. Purchasing Managers Index is declining. You will see all of
this [indiscernible] (00:25:07) but we are still shooting for 20% to 25% for the top line growth. But we expect the
lower business activity again in the autumn is slower theoretically. That said, new build slowing down
[indiscernible] (00:25:29). The energy efficiency initiative laws, targets et cetera to meet the climate goals that is
there [indiscernible] (00:25:43) see the decline as temporary and new build capacity will shift over to energy
efficiency action and there is a lot of money put to that [indiscernible] (00:25:54-00:25:59) is €11.5 billion, €13
billion a year. Italy is still going strong and the year target, the [ph] binding (00:26:05) targets are out at the
moment there. So, I think we see it's relatively shorter and on the EBIT, €100 million is substantial. Obviously,
with your prize inflationary, the kind of step-up in energy cost, electricity – mainly electricity and gas, this is
considerably north of the €100 million in the remainder of the year that impacts our margins. And we are
increasing prices compared to second-half after March to tune of 10% but at the end of the year, there is another
10% from what you pay for the average product. It's continued to climb the rest of the year, so non-stop another
10% which has progressed through the year. But if this level of gas and electricity prices are even worst, we
cannot adjust the pricing. We don't want to adjust the pricing up and down every month [indiscernible] (00:27:21).
Then we look at the investment level. It's not that we are slowing down investment except for responding,
[indiscernible] (00:27:30) maybe hold back a bit because we have capacity. We don't need more to invest. There's
more natural reasons, approvals, challenges to execute, normal type of things and I will say a little bit
exaggerated. I mean these challenges have increased the supply chain challenges. People worrying about if they
can produce liquid gas [indiscernible] (00:28:00). If that disrupt things, just go a little bit slower. And, yeah, I think
that's it.
So, with that I hand over for questions.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Morgan Stanley & Co. International Plc
speaker_turns.text:
Thanks, gentlemen. Two questions for me. So, obviously we have been or you've been surprised by the level of
energy cost inflation as we roll into the second-half of the year. Is there anything that you're doing on your pricing
or sourcing of energy costs? I know that some peers out there in the industry tend to look to secure energy under
longer-term contracts to have a little bit more visibility on their cost line. I know that that's not really been
something that you guys have done in the past but are you changing your full process around that?
And then the second...

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Yeah. I can...

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Morgan Stanley & Co. International Plc
speaker_turns.text: Sorry, go ahead. And then I'll ask my second question.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: No. No. Go ahead. Next question.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Morgan Stanley & Co. International Plc
speaker_turns.text:
So, on the top line guidance, you maintained that, but you are talking about the incremental price increases
coming through in the second-half. So, would it be fair to say your outlook on volumes has actually deteriorated?
And so while you've seen an increase in your pricing outcome, you've actually expecting to give that back in lower
volumes. And if that's the case, can you talk about which regions you've seen the greatest change in the volume
trends as we move into the second-half? Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. I would or can I start with the first one. We have had – I don't know 40 years, 50 years, whatever, forever
there's quite short hedging. If we hedge, we hedge a quarter when it makes sense – [ph] pre-buy (00:30:00). So,
coke, for example we have a framed agreement in that we negotiate quarter-by-quarter according to some rules.
So, generally we run short. And here some [indiscernible] (00:30:15) taken hedges when it's really obvious that
it's a good one, but we haven't had any of this strategy that we try to disconnect from the market forces with
power purchasing agreement or anything like that. So, this period we experienced about [indiscernible] (00:30:33)
then we can find a sensible hedge. They are dominant book but quite frankly even during the spring and Q2 we
benefited by not hedging because the SWAP market was better so, we haven't gotten around. So, I don't think
you can solve this with hedging. That's not possible. So we need to take a bigger risk around that and have a look
at the way we source in particular electricity and gas. And that's the homework we have to the [indiscernible]
(00:31:10) the different strategy on that and we are not done with that work but we are working on to see can get
a little less exposed from this when we have volatile clients [indiscernible] (00:31:24). Yes, we are looking at it.
The on the top line, we predicted actually from the beginning of the year quite low top line growth. We have never
been – we have never seen this year of double-digit top line growth but low single-digit volume growth. But it's
correct that we keep raising the prices. And we expect volumes to go down but I'm not saying that volumes going
down only because of [indiscernible] (00:32:07). I think that see a real impact into the market due to the
[indiscernible] (00:32:14), building materials generally have gone up, the projects are canceled, postponed and
there is a lot of uncertainty in the market and you'll also see lower activity. And I think we will see that progress as
we move through the year.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, SEB
speaker_turns.text:
Yes. Thank you. I have two questions as well. So first one, just some clarification on pricing. If I understand
correctly so it sounds like you're sort of reached a point where you feel you cannot continue to increase prices. Is
that how we should interpret this? So I mean if inflation stay at this level I'm not going to be able to raise prices
further next year?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
No. I'm not saying that. I'm not saying that. I mean, compared to the beginning of this year, let's say, Q4 to where
[indiscernible] (00:33:23) at end of the year, we're going to be up about 35%. But I was not feeling it because
within the scope where we – we were based on that scope from Q1 into Q2 because inflation happens, so we
know it's going to lag with our energy policy or the way we buy energy. We know it's going to lag on this
[indiscernible] (00:33:51). But here what has happened is that we have already launched our price increases. I
mean we are in Q2, Q3 now and we had already launched the increase for Q4 and I don't feel with the increase
we have launched that we can now come back two weeks after that, so three weeks after that and do any other
one for the same period. So that's [indiscernible] (00:34:20).

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, SEB
speaker_turns.text: Okay. Is...

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Yeah. Does that answer your question?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, SEB
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. It does. So, I guess my follow-up would be so even in a market where it's increasingly like the volumes will
go down, you still see possibility to continue to increase pricing when you do your pricing?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. I think it would be very [indiscernible] (00:34:48) in the market overall and we head into some sort of
recession, and then the renovation would kick in, I don't know how many months after but we have fairly big
demand that needs to be fulfilled. I probably will say that my strategy will be try to keep the margins [ph] on hold
(00:35:14) and try to protect that, and then take a bit [indiscernible] (00:35:17) on volume. Because really with this
cost level, you will need the price level to stay up because the cost level is extremely [indiscernible] (00:35:31).
And it's not that it's our cost in the company or things we can control is really this energy cost. That's very clear.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, SEB
speaker_turns.text:
That's very clear. Then my second question given the margin pressure you are seeing, have you considered to
take any actions on your fixed cost?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
We are. I mean, obviously, on the one hand we need to keep expanding the business. We need more capacity
even if there is a [indiscernible] (00:36:04) we need more capacity, because we have been so incredibly below the
last one-and-a half year. So we need more capacity and we need [indiscernible] (00:36:15). On the fixed cost, we
have a very good team. In most markets, we have our arms around attrition quite well even though it's up a bit.
So, have we [ph] utilized (00:36:30) this? I guess the really core things, we keep adding excellent resources. But
generally over the company, we are more restrictive. I mean it's much more restrictive on hiring and you have
attrition in the company, so that means we are working on fixed cost. And then things like travel and other things
they are 25% or more below the year [indiscernible] (00:36:55) new travel capture.
So, yes, we'll – we're starting to manage fixed cost already, preparing for the [indiscernible] (00:37:06-00:37:13).

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
Hi. Good afternoon. The first one is on gas situation. You have – and the prices have kind of obviously moved up
since July and as we look at the overall European energy prices up 20%. Does that mean that gives additional
risk to your 10% to 12% EBIT margin guidance for this year?
And a supplementary to that...

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: [indiscernible] (00:37:56)

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text: Yeah. Sorry. Carry on.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: [indiscernible] (00:38:06). Can you repeat the question to make sure I understand.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
No. See, the question is the energy prices since the end of July, on an average, have moved up 20% in August.
Now, considering that you are saying that you are not going to raise for the prices for Q4, which you have already
communicated in end of June. So, that means are we looking at a margin more like for the full-year at 10% rather
than upper-end of the range?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President, Chief Executive Officer & Member-Registered Directors, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
I see it like this, I'm just – we are quite good – we have been reasonably good with pricing and executing them,
reasonably good at running the business in difficult circumstances and also ramp up capacity.
What I see is that when you have these super erratic processes where geopolitical – geopolitics come in and can
change just between one month to the next, [ph] one energy site (00:39:13) with €20 million run rate and, yes, you
have a little bit more hedging, so therefore, I increased this [indiscernible] (00:39:23) to 10% to 12% to reflect that
there is – the biggest element in what's happening now, obviously we're going to have some volume impact to
this. But the big issue why we have that variance, that's the energy prices that jumps up Factor 2, Factor 1.5,
Factor 1, this is the variability issue. And therefore I want to tell you that it could be 10%, it could be 11%, it could
be 12%. It depends how these energy prices move.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. Understood. And my second question slightly on the longer-term basis and now the energy prices are in a
different league altogether, including gas. And as I understand with your SBTi-based target to 2034, you were

own product and put it back into production. We have a goal of achieving 15 countries by the end of this year. We
just launched Romania and Finland. And we are now – we have launched and we are now 17 countries already in
2021. So, we are ahead of that goal also.
Energy efficiency, on the right, this is the energy efficiency of our own offices, measured in kilowatt hour per
square meter. And here, we wanted to drop 75% by 2030 and 35% by 2022. We have just completed Hungary
and France. We are 19%. Before year-end, we need to reduce the run rate on [indiscernible] (00:04:13) 35%.

(00:04:57). So, that's good, and we have done it in a very evolving approach. We actually use our own

Water consumption, creating fresh water. We get water very cheaply, [indiscernible] (00:04:57) free almost
everywhere. But the [indiscernible] (00:05:16) said that with so many areas of the world running dry on drinking
water, fresh water, we have an obligation to do that. We have the goal of 10% reduction by 2022, and we
